stories.title: Massachusetts relaunches effort to redevelop Boston Government Center
stories.excerpt: The Boston Government Center is getting dolled up for a redevelopment — again. Gov. Maura Healey’s administration released an offering memorandum for the Erich Lindemann Mental Health Center and the Charles F. Hurley Building in Boston’s West End neighborhood, Bisnow reported. The parcels form a 6.5-acre office complex in need of a revamp. “We’re turning underutilized state property into new homes, preserving important parts of this historic site, and ensuring that critical mental health services continue uninterrupted,” Healey said in a statement. The 1960s-built buildings have suffered in recent years as they’ve aged. The Lindemann building alone is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Massachusetts relaunches 6.5-acre Boston Government Center redevelopment after 2024 collapse.
Previous $1B mixed-use plan (Leggat McCall, 2022) killed when life sciences market tanked.
New RFP requires affordability; 115 mental health beds must remain on-site or relocate nearby.
stories.summary: Massachusetts is re-marketing the Erich Lindemann Mental Health Center and Charles F. Hurley Building (6.5 acres, West End Boston) after a $1 billion mixed-use redevelopment by Leggat McCall collapsed in 2024 when the life sciences sector imploded. The 347,000-sf Hurley Building is vacant; the Lindemann center still operates 115 psychiatric beds. New RFP due summer 2025 will require affordability components and adaptive reuse. Capital needs exceed $300 million.
stories.impact: This signals Boston's difficult office-to-residential conversion pipeline. Life sciences collapse freed the site but also reduced speculative capital willingness; any winning developer will face hard affordability mandates and operational complexity (mental health services continuity), narrowing margins. For multifamily focused on dense urban infill, the slow, mission-constrained path here (vs. greenfield) shows why neighborhood-scale value-add plays remain more feasible than large civic repositioning in Boston.

stories.title: SVN | Chicago Facilitates Sale of Two-Story Industrial Property
stories.excerpt: SVN | Chicago Commercial facilitated the sale of an 18,000-square-foot industrial property at 3450 N. Kostner Avenue in Chicago for $1.43 million. Industrial Advisor Tim Newberg represented the seller in the transaction. The buyer, a Canadian millwork company, acquired the property to support its expansion into the Chicago market. The two-story building features high ceilings, heavy power, functional loading, and convenient access to major expressways and public transportation. “Infill industrial assets with heavy power, functional loading, and strong transportation access continue to attract significant demand,” said Newberg. “Properties like this are increasingly difficult to replace, making them highly attractive to owner-users seeking long-term operational stability.” The sale reflects continued demand for functional industrial facilities in Chicago, particularly among owner-users seeking space that can immediately support manufacturing and operational growth. The post SVN | Chicago Facilitates Sale of Two-Story Industrial Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Canadian millwork company acquires 18K-sf Chicago industrial property for $1.43M.
Buyer seeks expansion into Chicago market; heavy power, functional loading critical.
Infill industrial with irreplaceable functional specs driving owner-user demand and pricing.
stories.summary: A Canadian millwork manufacturer bought an 18,000-sf two-story industrial building at 3450 N. Kostner Ave., Chicago for $1.43 million to support its Midwest expansion. The property offers heavy power, functional loading, and direct expressway/transit access. The broker noted scarcity of such assets for owner-occupants needing immediate operational readiness.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. Story reflects tight Chicago infill industrial market and owner-user consolidation (not investment play), signaling healthy regional demand for manufacturing/distribution but not relevant to apartment development.

stories.title: Michael Puline: Principal’s Mindset is Essential to Modern Brokerage
stories.excerpt: Retail real estate veteran Michael Puline has joined Marcus & Millichap to guide the strategic growth of Marcus & Millichap’s and Institutional Property Advisors’ (IPA) retail divisions and lead the company’s retail investment sales professionals, helping to ensure seamless execution and client service delivery. He brings a 25-year track record of closing more than 2,500 leasing and sale transactions totaling $8.5 billion. Connect CRE sat down with Puline to delve into the factors that brought him to his new role. Q: You spent the past two decades on the principal side — running a diverse national portfolio at DLC, then nearly a decade leading retailer strategy inside a Blackstone-owned platform. What made now the right moment to cross over to brokerage, and how does sitting on both sides of the table change the way you’ll lead the retail division at Marcus & Millichap and Institutional Property Advisors (IPA)? A: I joined Marcus & Millichap and IPA because I believe we are at a pivotal point where human intelligence and, combined with advanced technology, will fundamentally reshape the marketplace. Given the firm’s leadership in transaction activity, it is the perfect platform for me to apply my expertise, skills, and relationships.  The decision to move now was driven by my belief that the brokerage of the future requires more than just execution-focused management; it requires a principal’s perspective. This means seeing beyond the property itself to understand the capital stack, operational challenges, and long-term business strategy. My goal is to help clients navigate complex operational, capital, and strategic decisions while bringing the added perspective gained from decades on the ownership side of the business. Q: Marcus & Millichap is the largest investment-sales firm in the country by transaction count, with a deep private-c
bullets: Retail veteran Michael Puline joins Marcus & Millichap after 25 years as principal.
Puline closed 2,500+ transactions totaling $8.5B; led retail strategy at Blackstone.
M&M betting on principal's mindset and tech-plus-services hybrid model in retail.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap hired retail real estate executive Michael Puline as head of retail investment sales, bringing 25 years of experience (2,500+ deals, $8.5B volume) spanning principal roles and a Blackstone-backed platform. Puline argues brokerages must blend principal's operational and capital-stack thinking with technology to stay competitive.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. Story reflects brokerage/service-provider consolidation and positioning in retail, not apartment demand, supply, or capital signals.

stories.title: Kalibrate’s CEO on Blending Services, Software in Location Analytics (VIDEO)
stories.excerpt: When Kalibrate CEO Charles Wetzel got his start in the location analytics business in the 2000s, it was almost exclusively a services business. More recently, software has come into the picture. “The nice thing with software is that software has allowed us to pivot and produce analysis much quicker,” Wetzel told Connect CRE in an onsite interview at ICSC Las Vegas. “A lot of people initially assumed that you could do it with software alone.   “But I think what we’ve found over the years is that while you theoretically can do services only or software only, if you can blend the two together and bring the art and science together and leverage the power of technology, the solution not only lasts longer but is more robust in the delivery,” he continued.  Watch the video below for Wetzel’s insights into the following questions:  How Kalibrate’s blend of expertise and technology outperforms standalone software or consulting An example of Kalibrate uncovering market potential that a restaurant or retail client might otherwise have missed How Kaibrate is evolving its data models and analytics to help clients anticipate structural changes instead of just reacting to them How Kaibrate is evolving its data models and analytics to help clients anticipate marrket changes instead of just reacting to them The post Kalibrate’s CEO on Blending Services, Software in Location Analytics (VIDEO) appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kalibrate CEO Wetzel emphasizes blended services-plus-software model for location analytics.
Software accelerates analysis speed; hybrid approach outperforms pure software or consulting.
Clients use data to anticipate structural market changes, not just react.
stories.summary: Kalibrate CEO Charles Wetzel discussed how blending location analytics software with expert services outperforms standalone models. Software speeds analysis; combining human expertise with tech enables clients to anticipate market shifts (e.g., restaurant/retail expansion potential) rather than respond retroactively.
stories.impact: Peripheral to multifamily development. Story is about tech/analytics vendor positioning, not about housing supply, demand, capital, or costs. May inform site selection rigor for multifamily developers, but no second-order market effect.

stories.title: Krusinski Construction Starts Work on 32K-SF Addition to Aurora Distribution Hub
stories.excerpt: Krusinski Construction Company (KCC) has begun the construction of a 32,000-square-foot addition to the Loaves & Fishes Community Services Food Distribution Hub at 580 Exchange Court in Aurora, Illinois. With this addition, the building will expand to 62,000 square feet. The 32,000-square-foot expansion will create greater storage capacity, improved distribution efficiencies, and enhanced collaboration capabilities with partner pantries through shared resources and co-buying opportunities. Members of the Krusinski Construction team include Greg Krucek, Senior Project Manager; Siddy Gupta Katkam, Project Engineer; Paul Gabriel, Project Superintendent; and Erin Anderson, Project Coordinator. Other members of the team include Powers Brown Architecture as the project architect and Cage Civil Engineering as the civil engineer. “The phasing of the project has been designed and will be executed to prioritize safety, minimize disruption to staff and volunteer operations during construction, and maximize the overall construction process,” said Krucek. The post Krusinski Construction Starts Work on 32K-SF Addition to Aurora Distribution Hub appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Krusinski Construction begins 32K-sf addition to Loaves & Fishes food distribution hub, Aurora, IL.
Expansion lifts total building to 62K sf; improves storage and operational efficiency.
Project prioritizes phasing to minimize disruption to food pantry operations.
stories.summary: Krusinski Construction broke ground on a 32,000-sf addition to the Loaves & Fishes Community Services Food Distribution Hub in Aurora, Illinois, expanding the facility from 30K to 62K square feet. The project increases storage, distribution efficiency, and co-buying capacity for partner pantries. Design emphasizes operational continuity during construction.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development. This is community-focused nonprofit infrastructure (food distribution); no bearing on residential demand, supply, capital, or costs.

stories.title: The Malin Establishes NoHo Presence with GFP Real Estate
stories.excerpt: The Malin, a hospitality-driven workspace operator, has signed a long-term lease for the entire fifth floor at GFP Real Estate’s 10 Astor Place in NoHo. The tenant will occupy approximately 19,400 square feet at the historic office property under a 15-year lease. The company plans to transform the full floor into a design-led workspace destination featuring private offices, dedicated desks, meeting rooms and hospitality-focused amenities. As part of the project, The Malin will also redesign the building’s lobby, extending its design language beyond the fifth floor. Together with the fitness amenity located directly below, the new workspace will contribute to a more cohesive ecosystem of work, wellness and community within the building, according to GFP. David Kaye and Neith Stone of GFP represented ownership. John Mears of Rue represented The Malin, which is expected to take occupancy in the first quarter of 2027 following completion of its custom buildout. The post The Malin Establishes NoHo Presence with GFP Real Estate appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: The Malin (hospitality workspace operator) signs 15-year lease for 19.4K sf in NoHo, NYC.
Entire 5th floor at 10 Astor Place; design-led amenities, fitness integration.
Occupancy Q1 2027; redesigned lobby extends brand ecosystem across building.
stories.summary: The Malin, a hospitality-focused workspace operator, leased the entire 5th floor (19,400 sf) at 10 Astor Place in Manhattan's NoHo for 15 years. The tenant will create private offices, dedicated desks, and wellness amenities; redesign will extend to the building's lobby. Move-in expected Q1 2027.
stories.impact: Not relevant to multifamily development. Story concerns office-to-premium-workspace conversion and hospitality branding, not residential supply, demand, or capital markets.

stories.title: Stockdale’s Andrew Saba On Medical Real Estate’s Supply-Demand Imbalance
stories.excerpt: Health care-related property has become one of the commercial real estate industry’s more in-demand niches and safe harbors, and now it’s become one of its more competitive hunting grounds. Los Angeles-based Stockdale Capital Partners has been building into that demand since launching its open-ended health care fund in 2023. Since then, Stockdale’s health care platform, led by managing director Andrew Saba, has expanded to other markets around the country including Phoenix, Austin, and Fairfax, Va. The thesis is relatively straightforward: Demand for outpatient care is still rising, the U.S. population is aging, and new supply has become increasingly difficult to deliver. Construction costs, labor expenses and capital markets pressure have pushed projects back or stopped them altogether, all while medical tenants need more space near patients and health systems. That imbalance has made medical outpatient buildings more attractive to institutional capital, real estate investment trusts and newly formed joint ventures. And Stockdale sees rent growth, strong occupancy, long-term leases and more competition for the best properties. Commercial Observer spoke with Saba about Stockdale’s national health care strategy, the supply-demand imbalance, and how artificial intelligence could eventually reshape the way health care tenants use space. The following has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: Tell me about Stockdale’s approach and your view of the market and the general fundamentals. Andrew Saba: High level, the fundamentals of the sector have not changed. We’re seeing a continued demand for outpatient space across the country, and our collective thesis is that that will be bolstered by the aging population that’s currently in place and is expected to peak over the next four to six years. Stockdale launched an open-ended health care fund about three years ago and has expanded across the country. Can you talk about how the strategy has evolved, or has
bullets: Stockdale Capital Partners expanding healthcare fund (launched 2023) to Phoenix, Austin, Fairfax.
Aging population, rising outpatient demand; new supply constrained by costs, labor, capital.
Supply-demand imbalance driving rent growth, occupancy, long-term leases; institutional capital flooding in.
stories.summary: Los Angeles-based Stockdale Capital Partners, which launched an open-ended healthcare fund in 2023, is expanding across Phoenix, Austin, and Fairfax, Virginia. The thesis: demand for outpatient care is rising (aging U.S. population) while new supply is choked by construction costs, labor constraints, and capital market pressure. Result: strong rent growth, occupancy, and lease terms attracting institutional capital and REIT interest.
stories.impact: Moderate indirect relevance. Healthcare real estate's supply tightness and capital inflow signal investor confidence in non-apartment commercial niches; however, it doesn't affect multifamily supply, demand, or financing directly. Note: healthcare's cited cost/labor constraints mirror multifamily challenges, suggesting economy-wide construction pressure.

stories.title: 2026 CMBS Cap Rates Range From 5.41% to 8.02%
stories.excerpt: CRED iQ analyzed $26.1 billion of the most recently issued loans securitized in 2026 across commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) conduit, single-asset, single-borrower (SASB), Freddie Mac and commercial real estate collateralized loan obligation transactions. The data exposes a market split cleanly in two. On a balance-weighted basis, the average cap rate on newly originated collateral now sits almost exactly on top of the average mortgage coupon, meaning the typical 2026 borrower is financing at roughly zero positive leverage. Where a property sits relative to that line depends almost entirely on property type. Within subtypes, the dispersion widens further. Super-regional malls priced at an 8.94 percent weighted cap rate — nearly 250 basis points (bps) above anchored retail centers at 6.48 percent — while garden multifamily (5.51 percent) and multifamily cooperatives (4.89 percent) anchored the low end. Property types financing at negative leverage. Every “favored” income sector is now borrowing through its cap rate. Manufactured housing (minus 86 bps), mixed-use (minus 43 bps), self-storage (minus 35 bps), industrial (minus 30 bps) and multifamily (minus 19 bps) all carry coupons above their going-in yields. Sponsors are explicitly underwriting net operating income growth — or betting on lower refinancing rates — to make the math work. In contrast, hospitality (plus 124 bps), office (plus 95 bps) and retail (plus 20 bps) are the only sectors still delivering positive leverage, compensation for the credit risk that lenders perceive there. How conservative is office and hotel underwriting? Extremely. Office loans that cleared the securitization market in 2026 carry a 13.8 percent weighted NCF debt yield and just 55.4 percent cutoff loan to value (LTV) — the most conservative credit profile of any major sector. Hospitality runs nearly identical at a 13.8 percent debt yield. Only well-leased, low-leverage office is getting financed, while everything else rema
bullets: CRED iQ analyzed $26.1B 2026 CMBS originations; cap rates range 5.41% to 8.02%.
Multifamily (garden 5.51%, coops 4.89%) anchor low end; super-regional malls 8.94%.
Favored sectors (manufactured, mixed-use, self-storage, industrial, multifamily) borrowing above cap rate—zero positive leverage.
stories.summary: CRED iQ reviewed $26.1 billion in 2026 CMBS loan originations across conduit, SASB, Freddie Mac, and CLO structures. Garden multifamily averaged 5.51% cap rate; cooperatives 4.89%. Super-regional malls hit 8.94%. On balance, average coupon now sits at cap rate—meaning typical borrowers have zero positive leverage. Favored sectors (multifamily, industrial, self-storage, mixed-use, manufactured housing) all carry coupons above cap rates.
stories.impact: Critical for multifamily development. Zero-leverage borrowing environment signals investor caution despite low cap rates; favored status (multifamily at minus 19 bps to par cap rate) masks thin margins for new construction. CMBS willingness to finance below cap-rate projects suggests capital still available for core multifamily, but underwriting tighter. Developers must achieve stronger operating assumptions or accept lower returns; financing spreads compressing, making development feasibility sensitive to cost control and achievable rents.

stories.title: Miami Wilds water park project crashes after judge nixes dev site lease due to “mutual mistake”
stories.excerpt: A controversial mixed-use water park project near Zoo Miami that’s been in the works for over a decade has collapsed, after a judge voided the development site lease.  The developers –– Paul M. Lambert, Bernard Zyscovich and Michael Diaz Jr. –– working through the entity Miami Wilds LLC planned a water park, a 200-key hotel and between 15,000 and 20,000 square feet of commercial and dining space on a 27.5-acre site they had leased from the county, according to Lambert. Miami Wilds was to revamp and manage another 38.5 acres of parking lots to be shared between the development and […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Wilds mixed-use project (water park, 200-key hotel, 15-20K sf retail) collapses after judge voids lease.
27.5-acre site near Zoo Miami; environmental litigation (endangered species, Pine Rockland habitat) caused decade-long delays.
Court upheld Miami-Dade County's argument that federal ruling reinstated commercial development prohibition.
stories.summary: The Miami Wilds water park and mixed-use development (water park, 200-room hotel, 15,000–20,000 sf retail on 27.5 acres near Zoo Miami) collapsed after a Miami-Dade Circuit Court judge voided the development lease. The project was mired in environmental litigation for five+ years; conservationists cited endangered species and Richmond Pine Rockland habitat protection. The county successfully argued that a federal court ruling reinstated a ban on commercial development.
stories.impact: South Florida development risk signal. The collapse reflects enforcement of environmental constraints on large-acreage infill projects. For multifamily developers in Miami-Dade, this underscores permitting and environmental due-diligence complexity; sites with habitat or wildlife concerns face steep litigation risk and timeline uncertainty, even after initial entitlement. Suggests greenfield or less-constrained infill parcels offer lower execution risk in South Florida.

stories.title: Belmont developer plans 202 apartments in neighboring San Mateo
stories.excerpt: The McLellan Company is moving forward with multifamily plans in San Mateo.  The Belmont-based developer has filed formal application permits for a six-story residential building at 4095 Pacific Boulevard in San Mateo, close to the city’s border with Belmont, San Francisco YIMBY reported. McLellan is listed as both the project applicant and property owner in legal documents, per S.F. YIMBY.  When complete, the development will deliver 202 units in a 79-foot tall structure at the corner of Pacific Boulevard and Laurie Meadows Drive, across the street from Caltrain tracks roughly halfway between the Belmont and Hillsdale stations. The edifice will […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: McLellan Company files permit for 202-unit, 6-story apartment at 4095 Pacific Blvd, San Mateo.
24 studios, 111 one-bed, 67 two-bed; 25 units deed-restricted below-market-rate.
San Mateo must deliver 7,015 new units by 2031; SB 330, density bonus streamline approvals.
stories.summary: The McLellan Company (Belmont-based) filed permits for 202 apartments (6 stories, 79 feet, 324K sf total) at 4095 Pacific Boulevard, San Mateo, near Caltrain stations. Unit mix: 24 studios, 111 one-bed, 67 two-bed; 25 deed-restricted affordable. Project benefits from SB 330 and state density bonus law. Demolishes existing gas station and retail. San Mateo has 7,015-unit state mandate through 2031.
stories.impact: Positive demand signal for Bay Area multifamily near transit. San Mateo's state-mandated housing goal (7,015 units by 2031) drives streamlined entitlements and density bonus incentives; McLellan's filing shows developer confidence in achievable rents and SB 330-enabled speed-to-market. Caltrain proximity strengthens demand fundamentals. Bay Area multifamily developers should monitor San Mateo permitting velocity; successful deliveries near transit could unlock more suburban infill sites within the growth mandate.

stories.title: ARCO Design/Build Takes 10K SF at Williams Equities’ 470 Park Avenue South
stories.excerpt: National commercial construction firm ARCO Design/Build has signed a 9,771-square-foot lease at Williams Equities’ 470 Park Avenue South, Commercial Observer has learned. The 15th-floor lease was signed in late March, according to a spokesperson for the landlord. A Colliers team of Andrew Roos, Michael Cohen, Mac Roos, Jessica Verdi and Rachel Kirkham represented Williams Equities, while Cushman & Wakefield’s Jason Kroeger and Pierce Hance negotiated on behalf of ARCO. The deal’s lease term and asking rent were not disclosed. A report from CBRE found office asking rents in Midtown averaged $84.79 per square foot in March, when ARCO signed its lease. The 18-story office tower, located on Park Avenue South between East 31st and East 32nd streets, was acquired by Michael Cohen’s Williams Equities in January 2025 for $147.5 million. The landlord has since leased 100,000 square feet of office and retail space, according to a spokesperson, including a 10,500-square-foot deal for marketing firm Dept. ARCO specializes in design and construction in the industrial sector, as well as self-storage, office and tenant improvement projects. The firm’s New York office, established in 2018 and led by Alex Edgar and Jim Wyatt, is based in White Plains, according to its website. It is unclear how the firm’s newest Manhattan office will be utilized. A call to ARCO’s White Plains office was not immediately returned. The firm performs work throughout northern New Jersey, Long Island and up the Interstate 95 corridor to Connecticut. Its past projects include a 585,000-square-foot Bronx logistics center and an Arizona Beverages production and bottling facility in Woodbridge, N.J. Spokespeople for Colliers and C&W did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: ARCO Design/Build leases 9,771 sf at Williams Equities' 470 Park Ave South, Manhattan (15th floor).
Williams Equities acquired building Jan 2025 for $147.5M; has leased 100K sf to date.
March 2025 Midtown office asking rents averaged $84.79/sf; lease terms undisclosed.
stories.summary: ARCO Design/Build signed a 9,771-sf lease on the 15th floor of 470 Park Avenue South (between E. 31–32 Streets, Midtown Manhattan) in late March 2025. Williams Equities, which acquired the 18-story tower for $147.5 million in January 2025, has now leased 100,000 sf. March 2025 Midtown office rents averaged $84.79/sf asking.
stories.impact: Not relevant to multifamily development. Story concerns Manhattan office leasing velocity and Williams Equities' repositioning strategy, not residential supply, demand, or capital markets.

stories.title: ESRT Picks Sevinc Yuksel as Senior VP of Design and Construction
stories.excerpt: Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT) has hired Sevinc Yuksel as its new senior vice president of design and construction, Commercial Observer has learned. Yuksel brought her 20 years of experience in the industry to the real estate investment trust (REIT) in June, according to her LinkedIn page, coming off a three-year stint as head of design and construction at Rudin. At ESRT, Yuksel will report to Jackie Renton, chief operating officer and co-head of real estate of the REIT. Renton herself arrived at ESRT in September of last year. In her new role, Yuksel will lead all design, construction and capital improvement activities across ESRT’s portfolio, the firm told CO. She’ll also play a critical role in new acquisitions. Yuksel did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Yuksel attended high school in Istanbul, Turkey, and holds an undergraduate degree in structural engineering and a master’s degree in construction management from Columbia University, where she finished her studies in 2008. She began her post-graduate career at Turner Construction Company, where her projects included 1271 Avenue of the Americas, Columbia University’s Northwest Science Building and JetBlue’s terminal extension at John F. Kennedy International Airport. Yuksel later joined Related Companies, where she oversaw construction activities for 50 Hudson Yards. Yuksel left Related in 2023 for Rudin. “We greatly appreciate Sevinc’s contributions to Rudin and wish her all the best in her new role,” a Rudin spokesperson told CO in a statement. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Empire State Realty Trust hires Sevinc Yuksel as SVP Design & Construction (June 2025).
Yuksel brings 20 years experience; former head of design/construction at Rudin (3 years).
Will lead design, construction, capital improvements across ESRT portfolio and new acquisitions.
stories.summary: Empire State Realty Trust hired Sevinc Yuksel (20-year industry veteran, most recently head of design/construction at Rudin) as Senior VP of Design and Construction effective June 2025. She holds a Columbia structural engineering degree and construction management master's. Will oversee all design, construction, and capital improvement activities across ESRT's portfolio and lead new acquisitions.
stories.impact: Not relevant to multifamily development. ESRT is an office/retail REIT; hiring moves signal REIT operational restructuring but no bearing on apartment supply, demand, or capital.

stories.title: Bachow seeks boost in density for downtown Fort Lauderdale tower
stories.excerpt: Bachow Ventures is looking to squeeze more value out of its Fort Lauderdale tower, proposing 62 additional units as part of a revised plan to increase the development’s density.  The Miami-based developer first secured approval in April 2023 for a proposed redevelopment of the 33,600-square-foot lot at 11 North Andrews Avenue. That proposal was for a 244-foot tall mixed-use development with 23 stories and 316 residential units.  In December 2023, the city approved an amendment to the proposal, allowing for an additional 14 stories reaching 385 feet tall and 1,500 square feet of commercial space.  Now Bachow has revised its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Bachow Ventures revises Fort Lauderdale downtown tower to add 62 units (378 total), shrink height to 293 ft.
Seeks density bonus: floorplate 12.5K→20K sf, tower separation 30→25 ft, setback 35→27 ft.
City vote June 16; total commercial space 4,800 sf. Site bought 2023 for $8M.
stories.summary: Bachow Ventures revised its downtown Fort Lauderdale mixed-use tower at 11 North Andrews Avenue, proposing 62 additional units for a total of 378 residential units while reducing height from 385 ft to 293 ft (27 stories). Density tweaks include larger floorplate (12.5K→20K sf), tighter tower separation (30→25 ft), and reduced setback (35→27 ft). 4,800 sf commercial space. City votes June 16. Bachow bought the 33,600-sf site for $8 million in 2023.
stories.impact: South Florida zoning/entitlement flexibility signal. Bachow's ability to reprogram (reduce height, increase units) via density bonus and design waiver flexibility shows Fort Lauderdale downtown is adapting zoning to capture more residential yield. This signals municipal receptiveness to densification and potential upside for multifamily developers holding downtown infill sites in Fort Lauderdale—setback/tower-separation variances may unlock marginal unit count gains and improved pro forma returns.

stories.title: Penthouse at 80 Clarkson Street In Contract With $80M Asking Price
stories.excerpt: Developers Zeckendorf Development and Atlas Capital Group, along with the Baupost Group, are about to set a record for the most expensive unit on a price-per-square-foot basis in Lower Manhattan. A duplex penthouse with an $80 million price tag has gone into contract at the under-development 80 Clarkson condominium building in the West Village at 80 Clarkson Street, the Wall Street Journal first reported. The unnamed buyer was represented by Christine Miller Martin and Kyle Blackmon of Compass. The brokers and a spokesperson for the developers declined Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. The unit, which spans 7,120 square feet of interior space and includes a 921-square-foot private outdoor terrace, is priced at $11,236 per square foot. The deal is Lower Manhattan’s most expensive contract, and the second-largest condominium contract signed in New York City so far this year, according to a source with knowledge of the deal. The largest sale of the year was a penthouse at 1122 Madison Avenue on the Upper East Side, which sold for $89.5 million. Other record-setting condo deals in Manhattan include Ken Griffin’s $238 million acquisition in 2019 of a penthouse at 220 Central Park South, the $115 million sale of the duplex penthouse at Central Park Tower in 2024, and the $60 million sale of a duplex condo at 150 Charles Street in 2025. Once completed, 80 Clarkson will be a condominium development across two towers standing 37 and 45 stories tall. It will include 112 residences ranging from two bedrooms to seven bedrooms. Residents will have access to 50,000 square feet of amenities, including an 82-foot lap pool, hot and cold plunge pools, a fitness center, a spa, a screening room, a golf simulator and a library. Since launching sales last year, more than $1 billion worth of apartments at 80 Clarkson have gone into contract, including a $129 million residence and another $75 million unit in contract, a source said. And the sale of luxury residential proper
bullets: Zeckendorf Development, Atlas Capital, Baupost Group selling duplex penthouse at 80 Clarkson (West Village) for $80M.
Unit: 7,120 sf interior + 921 sf terrace; $11,236/sf—record for Lower Manhattan.
Second-priciest NYC contract 2025 (after $89M Madison Ave); 22 total contracts filed; $1B+ total sales.
stories.summary: A duplex penthouse at 80 Clarkson Street (West Village condo by Zeckendorf Development, Atlas Capital Group, and Baupost Group) has gone into contract at $80 million. The unit spans 7,120 sf interior plus 921 sf private terrace at 400 feet elevation—highest residential unit in West Village history. At $11,236/sf, it sets a Lower Manhattan record. Second-priciest contract in NYC this year (after $89M at 1122 Madison). The project reports $1 billion+ in signed contracts across 112 units; 22 deals filed in recent amendment.
stories.impact: Luxury condo market strength in Manhattan, not multifamily. While 80 Clarkson's sales success signals high-end residential demand and capital willingness in West Village, it does not inform multifamily development feasibility, costs, or market conditions. Luxury condo and rental apartment markets operate separately.

stories.title: Zeckendorf, Atlas Capital’s 80 Clarkson inks $80M contract
stories.excerpt: Zeckendorf Development and Atlas Capital Group’s downtown condo project has scored another big deal. The project snagged a contract for a duplex penthouse asking $80 million in its west tower, the Wall Street Journal reported. If the deal closes at the price, it would set a record price per square foot mark for a home sold Downtown at $11,236.  It is also the second priciest contract signed in New York City this year, after a condo asking $89 million at Legion Investment Group and Nahla Capital’s project at 1122 Madison Avenue found a buyer.  At 400 feet in the air, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: 80 Clarkson penthouse contract ($80M) is 2nd-most-expensive signed in NYC this year.
Zeckendorf, Atlas Capital, Baupost Group project reports $1B+ in total signed sales.
Prior $129M multi-unit contract (Dec 2024) holds highest price in building.
stories.summary: 80 Clarkson Street's $80 million duplex penthouse contract is NYC's second-priciest signed contract in 2025 (after $89 million at 1122 Madison). The 112-unit West Village condo development has signed 22 contracts totaling over $1 billion in sales; a prior $129 million multi-unit deal (December 2024) remains the building's highest. Sales quietly launched over one year ago.
stories.impact: Duplicate/adjacent to idx 14. Luxury condo marker; no multifamily development relevance.

stories.title: Tech investor Bob Zangrillo revives $3B Little Haiti megaproject
stories.excerpt: Technology investor Bob Zangrillo is reviving plans for a $3 billion mixed-use project in Miami’s Little Haiti neighborhood, more than a decade after first unveiling the vision, Bloomberg reported.  Zangrillo is partnering with Miami-based Plaza Equity Partners on the proposed 7.8 million-square-foot development, an office hub aimed at companies in artificial intelligence, asset management and venture capital. The project would also include more than 2,600 residential units, a hotel and retail space. The first phase calls for the office campus, which Zangrillo’s firm, Dragon Global, would anchor, along with a 25-story tower with 349 rental apartments. In recent years, technology […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tech investor Bob Zangrillo revives $3B Little Haiti megaproject: 7.8M sf, 2,600+ residential units.
First phase: AI-focused office campus + 25-story tower with 349 rental apartments.
Palantir HQ moved to Miami-Dade 2025; Amazon, Apple, Gemini also expanded South Florida.
stories.summary: Tech investor Bob Zangrillo (founder of Dragon Global, early investor in Uber, Anthropic, SpaceX; pardoned by Trump) is reviving a $3 billion mixed-use 'Magic City Innovation District' in Miami's Little Haiti (7.8M sf total, 2,600+ residential units, hotel, retail). Phase 1 targets an AI/asset-management/VC office campus anchored by Dragon Global, plus a 25-story tower with 349 rental apartments. Parallels recent South Florida tech migration: Palantir HQ to Miami-Dade (2025), Apple/Amazon/Gemini expansions in county.
stories.impact: Miami-Dade demand catalyst for multifamily near emerging tech corridor. Palantir's HQ relocation, Zangrillo's AI-district vision, and adjacent Amazon/Apple/Gemini clustering signal potential for sustained job migration to South Florida and corresponding rental demand in Little Haiti/Wynwood submarkets. Developer window: if Zangrillo executes Phase 1 office + 349 units, it validates 'tech district' positioning; competing multifamily projects in Wynwood/Little Haiti may benefit from halo effect (visibility, talent in-migration, retail activation). Monitor office absorption and pre-leasing momentum to gauge rental demand upside.
